questionText: State the domain and range of the inverse function $y = \arcsin x$, and explain why the domain of $\sin$ must be restricted before it can have an inverse.

steps.explanation: A function can only be reversed if each output comes from exactly one input. Otherwise the reverse process would not know which input to return.
Over its full domain sine repeats values — for instance $\tfrac12$ is produced by both $30^\circ$ and $150^\circ$ — so as it stands sine is not one-to-one and has no inverse.
We cut sine down to the interval from $-\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ to $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ (that is $-90^\circ$ to $90^\circ$), where it increases steadily and so hits each value only once.
On this restricted piece sine climbs from $-1$ up to $1$, taking every value in between exactly once — perfect for defining an inverse.

steps.description: Read off the domain of arcsin
steps.explanation: The inputs of the inverse are the outputs of sine, so $\arcsin$ accepts exactly the numbers from $-1$ to $1$. Asking for $\arcsin 2$ is meaningless — no angle has sine $2$.

steps.description: Read off the range of arcsin
steps.explanation: The outputs of the inverse are the restricted inputs of sine, so $\arcsin$ returns exactly one angle between $-\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ — its principal value.
So $\arcsin$ has domain $[-1,1]$ and range $[-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}]$, a direct consequence of restricting sine to make it reversible.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $y=\arcsin x$ has domain $-1 \le x \le 1$ and range $-\tfrac{\pi}{2} \le y \le \tfrac{\pi}{2}$. Sine must first be restricted to $[-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}]$ so that it is one-to-one; otherwise repeated outputs would make the inverse ambiguous.
